category_summary:
The **Anthropic Fable/Mythos** release dominated discussion, blending excitement over capability with controversy over safeguards.

- **Ethan Mollick** offered the most-cited balanced take: Anthropic [is sincerely worried about misuse](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-8b9c55f04df2) of **Mythos-class** models yet has over-restricted **Fable**, while failing to convince the public. **Nathan Lambert** [criticized the uneven application](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-8f1aeb6eb68c) of safety domains as misleading and a dangerous precedent.
- Real-world usage data went viral: one engineering team detailed **tokenmaxxing** on **Claude Max** plans and [how Fable strains quotas](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-4bf4061290f0), while **levelsio** showcased [porting Return to Castle Wolfenstein](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-5d1d3de37bba) to multiplayer web in ~an hour.

Technical and product threads also drew strong engagement:

- A highly-shared speculation that **LLMs could optimize coding style** so [weaker models can complete codebase tasks](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-4940f8343d6a) led on originality and credibility.
- **Demis Hassabis** celebrated **DiffusionGemma**, [a text-diffusion model 4x faster](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-865e2657390c) than other Gemma 4 models. **Perplexity** launched **Deep Research** [as a native skill](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-1acbab9590e5) in its Computer agent on a Search-as-Code architecture.
- **Gary Marcus** pressed an AI-economy contagion thesis, flagging an [alleged OpenAI price-cut consideration](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-fb2e77a0e429) and [seven recent industry setbacks](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-3a7c2ff3ee17) (blocked SoftBank lending, German liability rulings).
- **Anthropic** announced **Claude Corps**, [a fellowship pairing 1,000 early-career people](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-57822d795a49) with US nonprofits, and **The Rundown** [detailed the heavily AI-wired](/?date=2026-06-12&category=social#item-b2ad0c85b20b) **2026 World Cup**.

items.title: It seems like LLMs could optimize coding style by exploring ways of structuring code so weaker and w...
items.content:
It seems like LLMs could optimize coding style by exploring ways of structuring code so weaker and weaker models can still successfully perform tasks in a codebase.

There are surely stylistic quirks that are peculiarly impactful to transformers, but I bet there would be a lot of overlap with human capabilities.

Optimizing for understanding should help even the top frontier models, allowing them to understand things “at a glance” without having to explicitly explore. There will remain “better” and “worse” ways to code.

items.title: Up until yesterday, our entire MTS team has operated under the philosophy of tokenmaxxing as much as...
items.content:
Up until yesterday, our entire MTS team has operated under the philosophy of tokenmaxxing as much as possible on Claude Max plans.

With Fable, this may no longer be possible:
- One of our team members hit his limit 3 times yesterday and used the equivalent of $1.5k in 10 hours
- Half of our team has hit quota limits on eng work

This era of tokenmaxxing may need to be restrained - or at least have clear guardrails defined. We are concerned about running Fable at API-based billing. If every engineer starts spending tokens at levels equivalent to headcount costs, our burn rate will meaningfully increase. 

Just as startups are starting to bake model routing into their core product, we will have to start thinking about model routing in our core engineering usage.

items.reasoning: High engagement, original real-world cost data on Fable usage, and a forward-looking argument about model routing economics from a credible founder make this one of the most substantive posts in the batch.

items.content_html:
Up until yesterday, our entire MTS team has operated under the philosophy of tokenmaxxing as much as possible on Claude Max plans.

With Fable, this may no longer be possible:

  • One of our team members hit his limit 3 times yesterday and used the equivalent of $1.5k in 10 hours
  • Half of our team has hit quota limits on eng work

This era of tokenmaxxing may need to be restrained - or at least have clear guardrails defined. We are concerned about running Fable at API-based billing. If every engineer starts spending tokens at levels equivalent to headcount costs, our burn rate will meaningfully increase.

Just as startups are starting to bake model routing into their core product, we will have to start thinking about model routing in our core engineering usage.

This tweet from 14 hours ago is on track to get about a million views. 

But here’s the thing: the c...
items.content:
This tweet from 14 hours ago is on track to get about a million views. 

But here’s the thing: the conclusion is true, but the tweet itself is already outdated.  

WSJ’s scoop that OpenAI is considering drastic price cuts - basically lighting money on fire to save customers –  is further sign of weakness, and indicator of how much trouble OpenAI is in.

And when they go down, they will likely pull Nvidia and Oracle and Coreweave etc down with them.

Things are unraveling fast.

items.title: The 2026 World Cup opens today in Mexico City, and it might be the most AI-wired tournament in sport...
items.content:
The 2026 World Cup opens today in Mexico City, and it might be the most AI-wired tournament in sports history.

How FIFA is leveraging the tech on and off the pitch: 

- An optical tracking system with 16 cameras per venue, capturing more than 150M data points per match.

- Offside alerts delivered as audio straight into assistant referees' earpieces, triggered the moment an attacker strays more than 10cm past the line.

- All 1,248 players 3D-scanned into avatars for broadcast offside replays.

- An Adidas match ball with a motion sensor reporting 500 times a second.

- Referee body cams in all 104 matches, with Lenovo AI stabilizing the first-person feed in real time.

- Football AI Pro, a generative-AI match analyst, handed to each of the 48 teams for pre- and post-match breakdowns.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: The Rundown details how the 2026 World Cup is AI-wired: 16-camera optical tracking, audio offside alerts to referees, player 3D avatars, a sensor-equipped match ball, referee body cams stabilized by AI, and a generative match analyst for all 48 teams.
items.reasoning: Concrete, wide-ranging real-world AI deployment in a globally significant event, well-detailed, though from an aggregator with moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: The Rundown details how the 2026 World Cup is AI-wired: 16-camera optical tracking, audio offside alerts to referees, player 3D avatars, a sensor-equipped match ball, referee body cams stabilized by AI, and a generative match analyst for all 48 teams.

items.title:
A tiny robot is gaining the skills to perform surgeries inside the body. 

Researchers at Nanyang Te...
items.content:
A tiny robot is gaining the skills to perform surgeries inside the body. 

Researchers at Nanyang Technological University built a millimeter-scale magnetic soft robot that can perform five different internal functions.

Measuring 4.4 mm at its widest, the robot is steered wirelessly by weak magnetic fields, crawling or rolling through tissue before switching tools in under a second.

A reprogrammable magnetic core lets it cut tissue, grip and store samples, dispense drugs, and heat tumors.

In tests on gelatin and chicken liver, over 99% of exposed cells survived.

Still confined to the lab, researchers say it could one day perform minimally invasive procedures, such as biopsies, targeted drug delivery, and localized heating of tumors, in confined regions of the body that are difficult to reach with normal surgical instruments.

items.reasoning: Genuinely novel medical robotics research with concrete specs and results, though still lab-bound and from an aggregator account with moderate reach.

items.title:
My most valuable AI agent does absolutely no work, and I mean that as the highest compliment.

For a...
items.content:
My most valuable AI agent does absolutely no work, and I mean that as the highest compliment.

For a while it was just me and my Chief of Staff, Simon. Simon runs six direct reports (Monica, Chandler, Phoebe...), each with their own sub-agents. I'd hand him a goal or task, he'd orchestrate it, work came back. Good, but things slipped, or we'd drift from a goal, or the same problem would resurface three times. 

No one was watching the work going "is this good?" or "what keeps breaking?" or "what did we learn?" and updating the system. 

So I gave Simon an assistant named Toby. All Toby does is watch and fix.

I love Toby.

(Not that way.)

He flags when we drift from our goals, catches recurring issues, and manages our memory. The whole system gets smarter instead of just busier. More effective instead of just more powerful. 

Most people building agents seem to stop at the doer (task completer) or the judge (task verifier).  
✔️ AI doers might draft your invoice reminder emails, run competitive research for you, narrow down a big vendor group for you based on criteria, run a forecast on your Brazil market
✔️ AI judges might review your code before you push or check your PPT draft against brand guidelines. Useful, but it only looks when you ask it to or gets triggered after task completion. 

A watchdog looks all the time, and tells you what you didn't think to check. It's a safety net, a second set of eyes, a bar raiser. 

All of this came through iteration (and I walked my AI Agent Mastermind students through all four iterations of my AI agent workforce and what I learned), but a massive unlock was splitting the "project management" work in two - orchestration and deep reasoning with one agent + monitoring and memory with another. 

I keep saying this but the boring bits of AI are some of the most important bits. I'm sure the AI labs will add this functionality into their systems, but for now, Toby is a huge leg up for my AI workforce.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: A detailed thread on multi-agent system design, describing a Chief-of-Staff agent with sub-agents and the addition of a monitoring meta-agent that watches for drift, recurring issues, and memory management to make the system smarter rather than just busier.

items.content_html:
My most valuable AI agent does absolutely no work, and I mean that as the highest compliment.

For a while it was just me and my Chief of Staff, Simon. Simon runs six direct reports (Monica, Chandler, Phoebe...), each with their own sub-agents. I'd hand him a goal or task, he'd orchestrate it, work came back. Good, but things slipped, or we'd drift from a goal, or the same problem would resurface three times.

No one was watching the work going "is this good?" or "what keeps breaking?" or "what did we learn?" and updating the system.

So I gave Simon an assistant named Toby. All Toby does is watch and fix.

I love Toby.

(Not that way.)

He flags when we drift from our goals, catches recurring issues, and manages our memory. The whole system gets smarter instead of just busier. More effective instead of just more powerful.

Most people building agents seem to stop at the doer (task completer) or the judge (task verifier).

✔️ AI doers might draft your invoice reminder emails, run competitive research for you, narrow down a big vendor group for you based on criteria, run a forecast on your Brazil market

✔️ AI judges might review your code before you push or check your PPT draft against brand guidelines. Useful, but it only looks when you ask it to or gets triggered after task completion.

A watchdog looks all the time, and tells you what you didn't think to check. It's a safety net, a second set of eyes, a bar raiser.

All of this came through iteration (and I walked my AI Agent Mastermind students through all four iterations of my AI agent workforce and what I learned), but a massive unlock was splitting the "project management" work in two - orchestration and deep reasoning with one agent + monitoring and memory with another.

I keep saying this but the boring bits of AI are some of the most important bits. I'm sure the AI labs will add this functionality into their systems, but for now, Toby is a huge leg up for my AI workforce.

items.title:
Three layers you need to run agent swarms at scale:
- Runtime: solved.
- Orchestration and triggers:...
items.content:
Three layers you need to run agent swarms at scale:
- Runtime: solved.
- Orchestration and triggers: solved.
- Coordination (how agents pick up tasks from each other, verify they have cleared a stage, and proceed): not solved.

Stripe called theirs Minions. RAMP called theirs Inspect. Both are internal infrastructure for running fleets of background agents. Both built from scratch.

@loujaybee says this shouldn't keep happening.

GitHub is a poor coordination layer for agents. Noisy, designed for humans, not built for hundreds of parallel pull requests.

Lou's fix: a CLI gateway that any local coding agent can invoke to check whether it has cleared its current SDLC stage and can proceed.

https://t.co/nFw2YbaXwe

items.title: I have to stop boring all of you with my game ports but I ported another game to web with multiplaye...
items.content:
I have to stop boring all of you with my game ports but I ported another game to web with multiplayer, after Quake 1 yesterday and Quake 2 today, I thought, what if I ask Fable to port Return to Castle Wolfenstein from 2001?

And of course it just did it with ease and put it at https://t.co/k6bCSeeDsQ with multiplayer working!

I'd play this game a lot and always this map mp_beach, it's like the WW2 Omaha Beach landing, you have to breach the wall with dynamite and get "ze sekret dokuments"

I asked it at 15:50 "do you think we can do RTCW?" and 18:18 I joined the game and could play it, so about 3 hours but I also went to gym in that time for an hour, so I'd say an hour total

What it did (in Fable's own words) is some crazy hackery that I could never, or it would have taken me probably a year, not an hour! 🤯🤯🤯
- Started from iortcw, the GPL source port of RTCW (ioquake3-based)
- Borrowed the emscripten patches from Wwasm (a single-player-only WASM port) — but it patches the SP tree, and RTCW's MP codebase diverges heavily (1,400+ lines different in some files), so I diffed Wwasm vs clean SP to extract the patch set, then re-grafted each #ifdef __EMSCRIPTEN__ block into the MP code by hand
- Compiled the MP engine to WebAssembly with emscripten + GL4ES (translates the game's OpenGL 1.x calls → WebGL2)
- Fixed what the graft missed: GL4ES init lived in SP's renderer file, MP uses a different one → "OpenGL 1.1 required" crash until I added initialize_gl4es()
to MP's R_Init
- Re-enabled networking (Wwasm disables it for SP) and patched out the fatal 1.41 point-release check
- Built the native iowolfded dedicated server from the same source (so client/server protocols + paks match exactly), systemd service on mp_beach
 - The 2001 CD ISO's data is buried in a Wise installer Setup.exe — Wine wouldn't install (i386 dep hell), so I used offzip to scan the binary for deflate streams and carved out the intact pak0.pk3 (316MB) + mp_pak0.pk3 (all 8 MP maps incl. mp_beach) + UI menu pak
 - Client fetches the paks over HTTPS into the emscripten filesystem before engine boot (held with a "run dependency"), mounted as IndexedDB (IDBFS) so the 380MB downloads once ever

items.summary: Levelsio describes using Anthropic Fable to port Return to Castle Wolfenstein from 2001 to a multiplayer web game in roughly an hour, detailing the iortcw and emscripten hackery involved.
items.reasoning: High engagement and a concrete demonstration of AI agentic coding capability on a complex porting task; useful real-world signal of Fable usage shortly after its release.

What it did (in Fable's own words) is some crazy hackery that I could never, or it would have taken me probably a year, not an hour! 🤯🤯🤯

  • Started from iortcw, the GPL source port of RTCW (ioquake3-based)
  • Borrowed the emscripten patches from Wwasm (a single-player-only WASM port) — but it patches the SP tree, and RTCW's MP codebase diverges heavily (1,400+ lines different in some files), so I diffed Wwasm vs clean SP to extract the patch set, then re-grafted each #ifdef __EMSCRIPTEN__ block into the MP code by hand

  • Fixed what the graft missed: GL4ES init lived in SP's renderer file, MP uses a different one → "OpenGL 1.1 required" crash until I added initialize_gl4es()

to MP's R_Init

  • Re-enabled networking (Wwasm disables it for SP) and patched out the fatal 1.41 point-release check

items.title: The core part of this Anthropic Fable release saga is that there are many overlapping issues at once...
items.content:
The core part of this Anthropic Fable release saga is that there are many overlapping issues at once. Some of which operate on different timelines of the AI arc, and some have easier fixes. In my critiques, I asked for specific changes to some things, understanding that some things don't have an easy fix.

The simplest issue was an uneven application of safety domains in a way that was misleading to users. This was an implementation issue that overlaps with a values-based decision of what their customers should be doing. Many people including myself pointed out how it was insane to list core safety areas and then have one of them launch with a different safety mechanism, one which actively mislead users. Doing this from the guise of safety was a major misstep and in my opinion Anthropic got very justifiably raked over the coals for it. Don't release the model if you can't hit your safety targets.

A subissue here is the idea of silent manipulation. This again is a horrible precedent, and quite odd for a company that has done extensive, leading technical AI safety research on ideas like CoT monitoring and other emergent misalignment issues. Silent manipulation of users is baking in a misalignment to the system at its face level. This comes with a permanent degradation in user trust, which begets a less safe environment for AI. Users who don't have clear information on how AI works will not develop safe working patterns with it.

The more complex issues are with how Anthropic handles broader scientific engagement with their models. The safety classifiers launched with these models obviously have accuracy issues to start. I have priced in that there will be more false positives to start, that's life. It's Anthropic's business to degrade their products at release time, or make the trade off of user satisfaction versus revenue. Still, it is a very real sign of concentration of power that businesses can make such obviously user-harmful behaviors and still lead in the market. This concentration of power is only starting to set in and we could see even weirder signs of it in the coming years.

It is now simple enough for me to test Claude Fable in my workflows and know if I'm restricted. This is obviously a suboptimal equilibrium – i want the best intelligence I can get, without restrictions – but it is easy enough for me to make sense of and work with.

The specific issue of restricting access to AI research in particular was a bubbling and hard to fix issue with Anthropic specifically, and the frontier labs generally. There is a common view that the frontier labs will be the mediators of all major scientific innovations in the future, as the places with the best models and the compute for inference to solve major problems. This is a categorical error in how science works, which is a community evolution of accepted ideas, and the the evaluation of your ideas by (hopefully numerous) independent, other practitioners. You cannot have science advance only within a monolith.

As an AI researcher I'm very sad to have the latest models restricted, but I would expect Anthropic to do this eventually. I lost more trust over the silent manipulation than I would with a restriction in access. Anthropic has made it pretty clear that they only trust themselves as the mediators of cutting-edge AI research.

If I had a say, Anthropic should've proactively made a program to make sure researchers get access in the broader AI community without the safeguards. Academics, nonprofit workers myself, etc. have no reason to not get access. The only valid argument here is that they want to control frontier AI, which is a know your customer part of serving these models.

This worldview of science has personally motivated me greatly over the last year, and increasingly so this week, to make the open science of AI continue to be viable. Olmo was a wonderful success here. Still, building research infrastructure is different from working for access to the tools needed to do the trade.

items.title:
Hot take on a bad day for OpenAI:

WeWork’s IPO fell apart.

I’ve been calling OpenAI the (possible)...
items.content:
Hot take on a bad day for OpenAI:

WeWork’s IPO fell apart.

I’ve been calling OpenAI the (possible) WeWork of AI for a long time.

I will not be surprised if their IPO plans apart, too. Their apparent pondering of “drastic” price cuts seem last-ditch, and will make the long term profitability story even harder to tell. The rejection of SoftBank’s second effort to get a loan against their shares is embarrassing, and says the banks aren’t buying OpenAI’s alleged valuation.

Big winner in this is probably Anthropic; should help their IPO.

And then the big question will be: can hold off Google?

I have no idea.

items.title:
A $40 billion payments company gave away its internal AI agent for free. 

Most AI agents lock you i...
items.content:
A $40 billion payments company gave away its internal AI agent for free. 

Most AI agents lock you into one model or vendor. 

Switching providers usually means rebuilding your whole setup.
 
Jack Dorsey's $40B company open sources an AI agent called goose.

It takes a different approach to this problem. It runs locally on your machine.
 
The project handles research, writing, automation, and code. 

Built in Rust, it ships as a desktop app, CLI, and API.
 
It connects to more than 15 LLM providers. 

You can even reuse your existing Claude or ChatGPT subscriptions.
 
Its extension system is built on the Model Context Protocol. 

That one open standard unlocks a lot:
 
> 70+ extensions for external tools
> Parallel subagents for separate tasks
> Shareable YAML workflow recipes
> Built-in prompt injection detection
 
The Linux Foundation now governs the project, keeping it vendor-neutral.
 
What happens when agents stop belonging to any single company?

> Parallel subagents for separate tasks

> Shareable YAML workflow recipes

> Built-in prompt injection detection

The Linux Foundation now governs the project, keeping it vendor-neutral.

What happens when agents stop belonging to any single company?

items.title:
You can now coach every AI action, not just the ending. 

Most AI systems train one model to do ever...
items.content:
You can now coach every AI action, not just the ending. 

Most AI systems train one model to do everything. 

The problem is forgetting.

Teach it a new skill, and older skills fade. They all share the same parameters.

MAPPA takes a different path. 

It trains many small agents together, each keeping its own weights.

But teamwork creates a blame problem. 

When a pipeline fails, which agent caused it?

A failure might show up at the last step. 

The real cause was an earlier agent forgetting to save a file.

MAPPA solves this with an AI coach. It watches every action as it happens.

The coach scores each step from 0 to 10. 

It reads roles, inputs, outputs, and error messages.

This gives feedback at every move, not just one signal at the end.

What you get with the open-source repo:

> Per-action scoring by a coach
> Accurate blame across agents
> Secure Python code execution
> Distributed training across GPUs

So if agents are the new scaling axis, who coaches the coach?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal explains MAPPA, an open-source multi-agent training approach that gives each agent its own weights, uses an AI coach to score every action 0 to 10 for accurate blame attribution, with secure code execution and distributed GPU training.
items.reasoning: Technically interesting multi-agent RL/credit-assignment method with an open repo, but low engagement and aggregator-authored.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal explains MAPPA, an open-source multi-agent training approach that gives each agent its own weights, uses an AI coach to score every action 0 to 10 for accurate blame attribution, with secure code execution and distributed GPU training.

items.title: LLMs are no longer created w/ human data alone. They rely on other models to generate & filter d...
items.content:
LLMs are no longer created w/ human data alone. They rely on other models to generate & filter data, evaluate outputs, & guide dev work.

So what is a modern LLM built on? Olmo 3 → 89 model + 183 dataset dependencies; Nemotron 3 → 273 + 560

We made ModSleuth to trace this. 🧵 https://t.co/1QvtmlxzYP

In December 2022, he was in the right place at the right time, but Anthropic is now eating his lunch, and Google is not far behind, after a slow start.

OpenAI’s board should have moved on a long time ago.

items.title:
Welcome to Apple. Where everything is carefully scripted.

That said, lots of Apple employees have t...
items.content:
Welcome to Apple. Where everything is carefully scripted.

That said, lots of Apple employees have told me the same.

Building for Apple's scale is much more difficult than being a startup and launching something on a weekend that isn't secure, is nerdy to use. 

Go to an Apple store and watch some of the classes people are taking. Many are still figuring out how to use their camera on their iPhone. 

Getting an agentic system into the OS will take a lot more thought than what OpenClaw or Hermes has put in yet, which are systems designed for early adopters/developers who know what they are doing.

It makes Apple seem slow and not innovative. I saw the same inside Microsoft when I worked there. Hard to do innovative things when you have a billion users who are on a spectrum of grandmas to nerds.

Then there is protection of their existing business models. I have a phone that has a completely agentic operating system on it, which takes away a lot of the business model of app stores and apps. 

Apple will take years to do such a thing, is my prediction. 

If you want such a thing (I do) then you gotta look elsewhere unfortunately. (It runs on Android since that OS lets developers do crazy things like that).

It doesn't seem like they really walked it back, though. I mean, you still don't get Mythos, er, Fable, for certain questions; it's just that now they're more honest about it. I hope I am reading that right.

My feed has quite a few still disappointed in this reply.

items.title: I think it's way deeper than cost efficiency. Several Apple employees have talked to me over the yea...
items.content:
I think it's way deeper than cost efficiency. Several Apple employees have talked to me over the years about "Apple scale." 

If you go and sit in an Apple Store and watch people taking a class, there are many Apple customers who are still learning how to use the camera on their phone. When they roll new technology into the Apple platform, it has to work for everybody, not just the nerds. 

I think that's mostly what he's saying: this technology is still too hard to use and too freaky for normal everyday people, and it brings new service problems to Apple. There is a cost efficiency part to it, of course, but it's really about making products that work at Apple scale. And how many users does that involve? Billions, right?
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: A longer reply arguing Apple's caution on new agentic technology is mainly about building for billions of non-technical users at scale, not just cost efficiency.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful, experience-grounded take on Apple's adoption calculus for agentic features; moderate engagement and reasoning depth.
items.summary_html: A longer reply arguing Apple's caution on new agentic technology is mainly about building for billions of non-technical users at scale, not just cost efficiency.
items.content_html:
I think it's way deeper than cost efficiency. Several Apple employees have talked to me over the years about "Apple scale."

If you go and sit in an Apple Store and watch people taking a class, there are many Apple customers who are still learning how to use the camera on their phone. When they roll new technology into the Apple platform, it has to work for everybody, not just the nerds.

I think that's mostly what he's saying: this technology is still too hard to use and too freaky for normal everyday people, and it brings new service problems to Apple. There is a cost efficiency part to it, of course, but it's really about making products that work at Apple scale. And how many users does that involve? Billions, right?

items.title:
I have tried every AI tool worth trying. Most hand you an answer. This one did the job.

I track the...
items.content:
I have tried every AI tool worth trying. Most hand you an answer. This one did the job.

I track the entire AI field for a living. The work that eats my week is not the thinking. It is the collecting. Reading, sorting, assembling.

I added Viktor to Slack and pointed it at my sources and my notes.

++ pulled the week's launches and papers
++ cross-checked them against what I already covered
++ drafted the shortlist with links
++ flagged two stories it thought I would have missed

Then it offered to format the whole digest. I approved.

Most AI tells you what to do. This one does it.

And not just for me. Justin Hibbert, CEO of Highgarden Holdings, ran a $12.5M quote through Viktor. It came back at $7.2M. The contractor settled at $8M.

If your job is keeping up, give it the collection work. Connect your sources, describe the digest you want, and approve what it ships.

items.title:
San Francisco comes through again. 

Spent a delightful hour with @khal_ism who is building a new br...
items.content:
San Francisco comes through again. 

Spent a delightful hour with @khal_ism who is building a new brain computer interface that you will hear a lot about later this year.

I wrote eight books about decade-long change coming. My first was about social media before this site were are all on started.

If I write another it will be BCIs. Have a list of neuroscientists and builders working in this field. It will be a trend of new things coming over next decade.

I already asked @elonmusk for a full neuralink. He said they aren’t ready for regular people yet, but told me “cognitive enhancements” are coming in three years.

OpenAI, Meta, and others are working on them too.

But now I know a lot more about how they work.

Thanks X. This morning when I woke up I had no idea who I would meet. Another meeting in a few minutes.

Can’t do this in most other places in the world. This morning I wrote that I had the day free, and already an amazing day. Plus the weather is so amazing today.

And my other surreptitious meetings today? Entrepreneurs who are changing how we work. Deeply too, more on that soon.

I already asked @elonmusk for a full neuralink. He said they aren’t ready for regular people yet, but told me “cognitive enhancements” are coming in three years.

But now I know a lot more about how they work.

items.title:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

Before obtaining a copy of this book, I had not yet become familiar with Claude Code and...
items.content:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

Before obtaining a copy of this book, I had not yet become familiar with Claude Code and didn't realize how robust AI Pair Programming is now. However, I did know and have rigorously promoted the absolute importance of Context (via Context Engineering) in any AI-prompted tasks, including coding. This book brings all of that together in an incredibly useful book (dare I say... a comprehensive toolkit and handbook) for AI-prompted coding in these multiple modalities: IDE, command-line, and pair programming. It covers memory management, agents, subagents, multiple-agent systems, context-sharing, orchestration, enforcement of coding standards, code maintenance, and more -- for executing AI-assisted development with confidence. I highly recommend this book.

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

items.title: I worked at Microsoft and interviewed 600 employees, from Bill Gates to the janitor. There are a lot...
items.content:
I worked at Microsoft and interviewed 600 employees, from Bill Gates to the janitor. There are a lot of smart people there, but when you put them in a committee, they become slow-moving and uninteresting. 

And that's the problem: Microsoft has let all the entrepreneurs leave and go somewhere else over the years. Now they have a lot of committees, and that's how you end up with this.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: A commentary arguing that Microsoft stifles talent through committee-driven decision making after entrepreneurs departed, framing this as a cause of slow, uninteresting output.
items.reasoning: Provides an experience-based organizational critique with moderate engagement, but is opinion rather than AI-specific analysis.
items.summary_html: A commentary arguing that Microsoft stifles talent through committee-driven decision making after entrepreneurs departed, framing this as a cause of slow, uninteresting output.
items.content_html:
I worked at Microsoft and interviewed 600 employees, from Bill Gates to the janitor. There are a lot of smart people there, but when you put them in a committee, they become slow-moving and uninteresting.

And that's the problem: Microsoft has let all the entrepreneurs leave and go somewhere else over the years. Now they have a lot of committees, and that's how you end up with this.

items.title: Claude Code is already powerful, but you can make it dramatically better and ~60% cheaper with this ...
items.content:
Claude Code is already powerful, but you can make it dramatically better and ~60% cheaper with this MCP server.

We tested the same speech-to-text benchmarking task two ways:

1. Using Claude Code alone
2. Claude Code + @withneo MCP

Same hardware. Same model.

Claude Code cost was $1.96, but using the MCP server was $0.74, 60% cheaper!
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino reports a speech-to-text benchmarking test where Claude Code plus the Neo MCP server was about 60% cheaper than Claude Code alone on the same hardware and model.
items.reasoning: Concrete cost-saving benchmark claim with good engagement, though promotional of a tool.

items.title: The @withneo MCP server makes Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and any other agentic coding system dramat...
items.content:
The @withneo MCP server makes Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and any other agentic coding system dramatically more efficient on AI/ML engineering tasks.

In the experiment we ran, using the MCP server ran 37% faster and produced a cleaner, more structured benchmarking pipeline.

The difference was approach.

Claude Code jumped straight to implementation using the standard libraries and design patterns most projects default to.

Neo, however, first researched the problem, selected the right libraries for this specific workload, and designed around performance from the start.

General-purpose agents become much more powerful when they can call specialized engineering agents.

You should take a look at the NEO MCP if you are trying to run evals, fine-tune models, inference benchmarking, or any other engineering workflow.

Here is the link to try it:
https://t.co/kGk44pN2qZ

And here is the complete case study if you want to read more about these results: https://t.co/rWHNsnvcaE

Thanks to the NEO team for partnering with me on this post.

At @Cisco Live, the conv...
items.content:
What does customer experience look like when the era of guesswork is over?

At @Cisco Live, the conversation went beyond technology to transformation.

The era of guesswork in customer experience is over.

At Cisco Live, I spoke with Carlos Pereira, Cisco Fellow & Chief Architect for CX, to talk about what "agentic speed" really means for enterprise leaders:
→ From visibility to action 
→ From reactive ops to resilient systems 
→ From pilots to production

What separates leaders from laggards right now? Courage.

Watch the full conversation 👇 https://t.co/jMhiCpsukd

#CiscoLive #CiscoPartner #CustomerExperience #CX

items.title: A model's lineage is broader than its training data, & every step can affect what – and how – th...
items.content:
A model's lineage is broader than its training data, & every step can affect what – and how – the final model learns.

Without provenance, it's harder to know where model dependencies came from, whether benchmark scores are accurate, & which upstream licenses/terms may apply.

The enterprise takeaway is simple:

AI performance is now a system-level challenge.

The winners will optimize chips, memory, interconnects, software, and architecture together.

Less latency means faster intelligence.
Less movement means lower cost.
Less waste means AI that can scale.

items.title:
The full text:

Kubla Khan

Or, a Vision in a Dream

In Xanadu did Kubla Khan
A stately pleasure-dom...
items.content:
The full text:

Kubla Khan

Or, a Vision in a Dream

In Xanadu did Kubla Khan
A stately pleasure-dome decree:
Where Alph, the sacred river, ran
Through caverns measureless to man
Down to a sunless sea.
So twice five miles of fertile ground
With walls and towers were girdled round;
And there were gardens bright with sinuous rills,
Where blossomed many an incense-bearing tree;
And here were forests ancient as the hills,
Enfolding sunny spots of greenery.

But oh! that deep romantic chasm which slanted
Down the green hill athwart a cedarn cover!
A savage place! as holy and enchanted
As e'er beneath a waning moon was haunted
By woman wailing for her demon-lover!
And from this chasm, with ceaseless turmoil seething,
As if this earth in fast thick pants were breathing,
A mighty fountain momently was forced:
Amid whose swift half-intermitted burst
Huge fragments vaulted like rebounding hail,
Or chaffy grain beneath the thresher's flail:
And mid these dancing rocks at once and ever
It flung up momently the sacred river.
Five miles meandering with a mazy motion
Through wood and dale the sacred river ran,
Then reached the caverns measureless to man,
And sank in tumult to a lifeless ocean;
And 'mid this tumult Kubla heard from far
Ancestral voices prophesying war!
The shadow of the dome of pleasure
Floated midway on the waves;
Where was heard the mingled measure
From the fountain and the caves.
It was a miracle of rare device,
A sunny pleasure-dome with caves of ice!
A damsel with a dulcimer
In a vision once I saw:
It was an Abyssinian maid
And on her dulcimer she played,
Singing of Mount Abora.
Could I revive within me
Her symphony and song,
To such a deep delight 'twould win me,
That with music loud and long,
I would build that dome in air,
That sunny dome! those caves of ice!
And all who heard should see them there,
And all should cry, Beware! Beware!
His flashing eyes, his floating hair!
Weave a circle round him thrice,
And close your eyes with holy dread
For he on honey-dew hath fed,
And drunk the milk of Paradise.

*** Original ends***

And even as they wove the ring,
And even as they ceased to sing,
I heard — O faint at first and far
As under ice the river's jar —
The dulcimer! the dulcimer!
And all my blood made answer to her:
Her symphony, her song, returned!
Within me all Abora burned;
And loud and long the music grew,
And out of empty air it drew —
Drew dome and dale and sunny ground,
And wall and tower girdled round,
Till they that wove the circle there
Beheld the dome ascend the air:
The shadow first, and then the gleam,
Then all the substance of the dream;
And cried no more Beware! but stood
Like children in a charmèd wood;
For there it hung, the rare device,
That sunny dome! those caves of ice!
And through the dome, and round, and under,
Ran Alph the river, soft with thunder.
It was the hour when Xanadu
Lay steeped in moonlight and in dew;
When every incense-bearing tree
Distilled its sweetness silently,
And, white above the chasm's gloom,
The fountain tossed its ghostly bloom.
Then walked the Khan in slow delight
Along the marges of the night,
And watched the shadow of his dome
Lie midway on the moving foam,
And heard beneath the fretted floor
The sacred river's buried roar,
And, ever mingled with its fall,
The voices of his fathers call —
Now near, now far, now almost plain,
Now drowned beneath the flood again —
And ever, from the caves of ice,
One voice that cried, and cried it thrice:
"The dome shall stand while stands the song;
The sword is short, the song is long:
What Kubla builds of stone shall be
The spoil of sword and snow and sea,
But what is built of song shall stand
When sea and snow and sword are sand."
So twice ten summers girdled round
With gold the towers and fertile ground;
But in the waning of the last,
The watchman heard a trumpet blast
Beyond the bastions of the snow,
And morning showed the moving foe:
Out of the north the horsemen came
With hoofs of thunder, eyes of flame;
Ten thousand banners, black and gold,
Poured down the passes of the cold;
And he who led them none had seen:
A shadow where a face had been;
And where he rode the grasses paled,
And the high noon above was veiled.
By that deep chasm's cedarn cover,
Where wailed the woman for her lover,
Now wailed ten thousand; hail on hail
The arrows mocked the thresher's flail;
The engines slung their vaulting stone
Like fragments from the fountain thrown;
And all day long, with mazy motion,
Red ran the river to the ocean.
Three days the girdled walls held good;
Three nights, about the haunted wood,
The watch-fires of the faceless king
Burned round the dome in narrowing ring;
And on the fourth, at fall of day,
The gates of Xanadu gave way.
Then, as his shadow crossed the sill,
The mighty fountain first stood still —
Stood still! and through the holy ground
There went a hush more dread than sound:
The earth, that long had seemed to breathe
In fast thick pants the turf beneath,
Held in her breath; and every rill
Hung in mid-motion, bright and still.
Then with a sigh from every cave
Rose Alph the sacred, wave on wave,
And round the dome of pleasure wound
His sinuous arms without a sound,
And drew it, dale and tower and all,
Down, as the dews of evening fall —
Not as a city falls to flame,
But as a dream, the way it came;
And caverns measureless to man
Received the dome — received the Khan.
And when the slow grey morning broke
There was no city and no smoke,
Nor wall, nor tower, nor incense-tree,
Nor sign of what was wont to be:
Only the ancient hills, and wan
Sweet meadows where the river ran;
And from beneath them, faint and clear,
A mingled measure rose; for fear
The horsemen wheeled, the host gave ground,
And fled the unconquerable sound;
And he that had no face to show
Turned, and was mist, and mixed with snow.
But far beneath the lifeless main
The dome of pleasure stands again —
Whole, as it sank; and girdled round
With twice five miles of glimmering ground;
And there the gardens, sweet as sleep,
Burn green and blue along the deep,
And forests ancient as the hills
Enfold the slow phosphoric rills;
For of itself the dome gives light,
A noon within the ocean's night,
A sunny dome no sun has lit,
With caves of ice beneath it knit;
And there the Khan, his wars all done,
Walks in the light that needs no sun,
And hears, more near than memory,
A music moving through the sea:
For there the Abyssinian maid
Plays ever, as of old she played,
And, wave by wave and year by year,
Makes all the deep a dulcimer.
And when on earth the moon is high,
And the long tides go yearning by,
Her measure, rising through the foam,
Goes forth to call the dreamers home:
It finds the gate of horn, and streams
Into the haunted house of dreams;
And whoso hears it, half awake,
Must sing thereafter for its sake,
And, waking, labour all life long
To build its likeness into song;
And mariners on midnight seas,
That hear faint strings upon the breeze,
Have leaned, and seen beneath the spray
A sunrise where no sunrise lay.
And therefore, in a vision, I
Beheld that damsel: this is why
Her symphony and song could win me,
And the dead music wake within me;
For, rising from the sunken dome,
Her measure crossed the midnight foam,
And climbed the cliffs of sleep, and found
A dreamer upon English ground.
And I have fed, and I have drunk;
The song that with the city sunk
Is risen in me, loud and long,
And all the dome is in the song.
So weave the circle round me thrice,
But close your eyes in bliss, not dread:
The milk of Paradise is shed
On all who hear; the caves of ice
Are cool upon the brows of men;
The dome is in the air again;
And when my song and I are gone,
Down in the deep it will shine on:
For, ancient as the hills, the sea
Enfolds that sunny mystery,
And there, till sea and song are done,
The sunless deep enfolds a sun.

Where Alph, the sacred river, ran

Through caverns measureless to man

Down to a sunless sea.

So twice five miles of fertile ground

With walls and towers were girdled round;

And there were gardens bright with sinuous rills,

Where blossomed many an incense-bearing tree;

And here were forests ancient as the hills,

Enfolding sunny spots of greenery.

But oh! that deep romantic chasm which slanted

Down the green hill athwart a cedarn cover!

A savage place! as holy and enchanted

As e'er beneath a waning moon was haunted

By woman wailing for her demon-lover!

And from this chasm, with ceaseless turmoil seething,

As if this earth in fast thick pants were breathing,

A mighty fountain momently was forced:

Amid whose swift half-intermitted burst

Huge fragments vaulted like rebounding hail,

Or chaffy grain beneath the thresher's flail:

And mid these dancing rocks at once and ever

It flung up momently the sacred river.

Five miles meandering with a mazy motion

Through wood and dale the sacred river ran,

Then reached the caverns measureless to man,

And sank in tumult to a lifeless ocean;

And 'mid this tumult Kubla heard from far

Ancestral voices prophesying war!

The shadow of the dome of pleasure

Floated midway on the waves;

Where was heard the mingled measure

From the fountain and the caves.

It was a miracle of rare device,

A sunny pleasure-dome with caves of ice!

A damsel with a dulcimer

In a vision once I saw:

It was an Abyssinian maid

And on her dulcimer she played,

Singing of Mount Abora.

Could I revive within me

Her symphony and song,

To such a deep delight 'twould win me,

That with music loud and long,

I would build that dome in air,

That sunny dome! those caves of ice!

And all who heard should see them there,

And all should cry, Beware! Beware!

His flashing eyes, his floating hair!

Weave a circle round him thrice,

And close your eyes with holy dread

For he on honey-dew hath fed,

And drunk the milk of Paradise.

* Original ends*

And even as they wove the ring,

And even as they ceased to sing,

I heard — O faint at first and far

As under ice the river's jar —

The dulcimer! the dulcimer!

And all my blood made answer to her:

Her symphony, her song, returned!

Within me all Abora burned;

And loud and long the music grew,

And out of empty air it drew —

Drew dome and dale and sunny ground,

And wall and tower girdled round,

Till they that wove the circle there

Beheld the dome ascend the air:

The shadow first, and then the gleam,

Then all the substance of the dream;

And cried no more Beware! but stood

Like children in a charmèd wood;

For there it hung, the rare device,

That sunny dome! those caves of ice!

And through the dome, and round, and under,

Ran Alph the river, soft with thunder.

It was the hour when Xanadu

Lay steeped in moonlight and in dew;

When every incense-bearing tree

Distilled its sweetness silently,

And, white above the chasm's gloom,

The fountain tossed its ghostly bloom.

Then walked the Khan in slow delight

Along the marges of the night,

And watched the shadow of his dome

Lie midway on the moving foam,

And heard beneath the fretted floor

The sacred river's buried roar,

And, ever mingled with its fall,

The voices of his fathers call —

Now near, now far, now almost plain,

Now drowned beneath the flood again —

And ever, from the caves of ice,

One voice that cried, and cried it thrice:

"The dome shall stand while stands the song;

The sword is short, the song is long:

What Kubla builds of stone shall be

The spoil of sword and snow and sea,

But what is built of song shall stand

When sea and snow and sword are sand."

So twice ten summers girdled round

With gold the towers and fertile ground;

But in the waning of the last,

The watchman heard a trumpet blast

Beyond the bastions of the snow,

And morning showed the moving foe:

Out of the north the horsemen came

With hoofs of thunder, eyes of flame;

Ten thousand banners, black and gold,

Poured down the passes of the cold;

And he who led them none had seen:

A shadow where a face had been;

And where he rode the grasses paled,

And the high noon above was veiled.

By that deep chasm's cedarn cover,

Where wailed the woman for her lover,

Now wailed ten thousand; hail on hail

The arrows mocked the thresher's flail;

The engines slung their vaulting stone

Like fragments from the fountain thrown;

And all day long, with mazy motion,

Red ran the river to the ocean.

Three days the girdled walls held good;

Three nights, about the haunted wood,

The watch-fires of the faceless king

Burned round the dome in narrowing ring;

And on the fourth, at fall of day,

The gates of Xanadu gave way.

Then, as his shadow crossed the sill,

The mighty fountain first stood still —

Stood still! and through the holy ground

There went a hush more dread than sound:

The earth, that long had seemed to breathe

In fast thick pants the turf beneath,

Held in her breath; and every rill

Hung in mid-motion, bright and still.

Then with a sigh from every cave

Rose Alph the sacred, wave on wave,

And round the dome of pleasure wound

His sinuous arms without a sound,

And drew it, dale and tower and all,

Down, as the dews of evening fall —

Not as a city falls to flame,

But as a dream, the way it came;

And caverns measureless to man

Received the dome — received the Khan.

And when the slow grey morning broke

There was no city and no smoke,

Nor wall, nor tower, nor incense-tree,

Nor sign of what was wont to be:

Only the ancient hills, and wan

Sweet meadows where the river ran;

And from beneath them, faint and clear,

A mingled measure rose; for fear

The horsemen wheeled, the host gave ground,

And fled the unconquerable sound;

And he that had no face to show

Turned, and was mist, and mixed with snow.

But far beneath the lifeless main

The dome of pleasure stands again —

Whole, as it sank; and girdled round

With twice five miles of glimmering ground;

And there the gardens, sweet as sleep,

Burn green and blue along the deep,

And forests ancient as the hills

Enfold the slow phosphoric rills;

For of itself the dome gives light,

A noon within the ocean's night,

A sunny dome no sun has lit,

With caves of ice beneath it knit;

And there the Khan, his wars all done,

Walks in the light that needs no sun,

And hears, more near than memory,

A music moving through the sea:

For there the Abyssinian maid

Plays ever, as of old she played,

And, wave by wave and year by year,

Makes all the deep a dulcimer.

And when on earth the moon is high,

And the long tides go yearning by,

Her measure, rising through the foam,

Goes forth to call the dreamers home:

It finds the gate of horn, and streams

Into the haunted house of dreams;

And whoso hears it, half awake,

Must sing thereafter for its sake,

And, waking, labour all life long

To build its likeness into song;

And mariners on midnight seas,

That hear faint strings upon the breeze,

Have leaned, and seen beneath the spray

A sunrise where no sunrise lay.

And therefore, in a vision, I

Beheld that damsel: this is why

Her symphony and song could win me,

And the dead music wake within me;

For, rising from the sunken dome,

Her measure crossed the midnight foam,

And climbed the cliffs of sleep, and found

A dreamer upon English ground.

And I have fed, and I have drunk;

The song that with the city sunk

Is risen in me, loud and long,

And all the dome is in the song.

So weave the circle round me thrice,

But close your eyes in bliss, not dread:

The milk of Paradise is shed

On all who hear; the caves of ice

Are cool upon the brows of men;

The dome is in the air again;

And when my song and I are gone,

Down in the deep it will shine on:

For, ancient as the hills, the sea

Enfolds that sunny mystery,

And there, till sea and song are done,

The sunless deep enfolds a sun.

items.title: Given an overall anti-GenAI stance among European bureaucrats, I wouldn't be surprised if the EU eve...
items.content:
Given an overall anti-GenAI stance among European bureaucrats, I wouldn't be surprised if the EU eventually makes a law about this.

It's indeed dangerous to let millions of small lies slip into these "overviews." Most people don't seriously verify each piece of the text, so these lies might accumulate in people's brains until some catastrophic critical mass is reached.

items.title:
One concept that makes this practical is LogicFolding.

Traditional chips spread logic across a flat...
items.content:
One concept that makes this practical is LogicFolding.

Traditional chips spread logic across a flat surface.

LogicFolding brings related logic closer together by moving toward more 3D structures.

Less distance means less delay.

And in AI workloads, small delays compound fast.

items.title:
This is the idea behind Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her’s Law.

Instead of asking onl...
items.content:
This is the idea behind Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her’s Law.

Instead of asking only, “How small can the chip get?”

We now have to ask:

→ Where is time being lost?
→ Where is data waiting?
→ Where are signals traveling too far?
→ Where is the system creating friction?
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: A sponsored post coining a Tau Scaling Law (Her's Law) that reframes chip optimization around where time, data waiting, and signal travel create friction.
items.reasoning: Introduces a branded scaling concept but within promotional content and with negligible engagement.

items.title:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors
→ Increase density
→ Improve p...
items.content:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors
→ Increase density
→ Improve performance
→ Lower cost

Moore’s Law powered the modern computing era.

But that curve is slowing.

The next optimization target is not only geometry.

It is latency.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: A sponsored post noting Moore's Law is slowing and the next optimization target is latency, not just transistor geometry.
items.reasoning: Reasonable hardware-trend framing but promotional and zero engagement.
items.summary_html: A sponsored post noting Moore's Law is slowing and the next optimization target is latency, not just transistor geometry.
items.content_html:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors

→ Increase density

→ Improve performance

→ Lower cost

Moore’s Law powered the modern computing era.

But that curve is slowing.

The next optimization target is not only geometry.

It is latency.

items.title: Yeah, yeah, yeah. The first Macintosh didn't sell, and Steve Jobs got fired because of that. The fir...
items.content:
Yeah, yeah, yeah. The first Macintosh didn't sell, and Steve Jobs got fired because of that. The first Newton didn't sell either, but both set up all their future product lines. 

That's what people aren't getting: this is all about setting up the next few decades of products.

And there it is: very successful. It is an amazing product to wear, it's just too heavy and too expensive. 

When they solve those problems, it'll start selling like hotcakes—particularly if they put Formula 1 into it. And guess what? Apple has an exclusive on Formula 1; nobody else is going to be able to get it.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Frames Vision Pro like the early Macintosh and Newton, as a foundation for future products that will sell once lighter and cheaper, citing Apple's Formula 1 exclusivity.
items.reasoning: Detailed Apple product strategy take but minimal engagement and tangential to AI.
items.summary_html: Frames Vision Pro like the early Macintosh and Newton, as a foundation for future products that will sell once lighter and cheaper, citing Apple's Formula 1 exclusivity.
items.content_html:
Yeah, yeah, yeah. The first Macintosh didn't sell, and Steve Jobs got fired because of that. The first Newton didn't sell either, but both set up all their future product lines.

That's what people aren't getting: this is all about setting up the next few decades of products.

And there it is: very successful. It is an amazing product to wear, it's just too heavy and too expensive.

When they solve those problems, it'll start selling like hotcakes—particularly if they put Formula 1 into it. And guess what? Apple has an exclusive on Formula 1; nobody else is going to be able to get it.

items.title: That is not true if you study Apple. The fact that an executive from Apple was allowed to come and s...
items.content:
That is not true if you study Apple. The fact that an executive from Apple was allowed to come and speak to the press, or an influencer (or whatever the hell you call Justin), says volumes about the fact that they're willing to stick with this product for a while. 

They would not have allowed that if the product was being killed.

Just the fact that he's talking says volumes to me. I've been studying Apple for a long fucking time. I was Apple's first child laborer (well, maybe second) and I was the first to buy an iPhone at Steve Jobs' store. Siri was even launched in my house.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Argues that Apple letting an executive speak publicly signals commitment to the Vision Pro product line, drawing on the author's long history covering Apple.
items.reasoning: Insider-flavored Apple analysis but very low engagement and off the central AI topic.
items.summary_html: Argues that Apple letting an executive speak publicly signals commitment to the Vision Pro product line, drawing on the author's long history covering Apple.
items.content_html:
That is not true if you study Apple. The fact that an executive from Apple was allowed to come and speak to the press, or an influencer (or whatever the hell you call Justin), says volumes about the fact that they're willing to stick with this product for a while.

They would not have allowed that if the product was being killed.

Just the fact that he's talking says volumes to me. I've been studying Apple for a long fucking time. I was Apple's first child laborer (well, maybe second) and I was the first to buy an iPhone at Steve Jobs' store. Siri was even launched in my house.

items.title:
When a shopper asks a question, they're showing buying intent.

- "What size should I get?"
- "How f...
items.content:
When a shopper asks a question, they're showing buying intent.

- "What size should I get?"
- "How fast is shipping?"
- "Do you have a discount?"

That question is a revenue signal. Most brands miss it.

items.content_html:
When a shopper asks a question, they're showing buying intent.

  • "What size should I get?"
  • "How fast is shipping?"
  • "Do you have a discount?"

That question is a revenue signal. Most brands miss it.
